speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Oh, no. They still click in front of the space.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. But they've got the things going now on-- on-- they have numbers automatic now. They got batteries in them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. I don't know. A guy still yells on his show.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but they still have shows that you can-- you don't have to put all the writing in there, you don't put seconds [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, that's right. That's right. Yeah, that's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Take number three. My name is Myra Slotnick and I'm interviewing Harold Blitzer on September the 5th, 1996 in Las Vegas, Nevada. Harold, tell me about the inspections of the different Red Crosses in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. We knew something was up because we were highly overcrowded there. We had I think at one time it was 75,000 people in. And all of a sudden, every so often, another transport is going. And we were considered Danish prisoners. We were never aware of it till after the war, we were exempt from any transport. And I'm very grateful to the government of Denmark, who kept us in-- kept us protected in some sense protected.

speaker: interviewer
text: And considering we were not even Danish citizens, we were just-- theoretically, we were actually refugees in Denmark, right? So we were told there was a Swiss Red Cross inspection coming up. So what they did at that time was they cleaned up like a street. There was a street with a front, like. They put a cafe house in there, then they put a bandstand for the orchestra-- they had a beautiful orchestra. Then they had signs-- grocery store-- and then they had signs on a bank.

speaker: interviewee
text: They issued money. Of course, you couldn't do anything with it, with the-- with the money. That was just like the show at the-- they had a post office. And all of a sudden, we got packages-- not everybody. Only the Danish-- Danish prisoners got all a sudden a Red Cross package from-- from the Danish Red Cross. And there was some food in it, but mainly it was clothing and vitamin pills. And I didn't know what a vitamin pill was and I traded it off to a baker.

speaker: interviewer
text: I says, I want a half a loaf every day, and forever and forever. And-- and I only lasted with this guy 10-- 10 days because he got-- he got shipped out to Auschwitz, poor bastard. So-- but at least-- and then people said, how can you sell vitamins? You've got no vitamins, your teeth are falling out, you know. Like you know, you could have taken your-- there was absolutely no value in the food we were getting. And people explained to me, you were crazy to give this guy the vitamins, you should-- you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, my stomach is hungry and I wanted the bread. So-- so-- so they cleaned up the city. They started planting flowers and-- and they had the women scrub the-- the sidewalks and everything. And they had this one particular street near the-- where it was really cleaned up and that's where they took the-- they took the inspectors. I at that time, when all the people got shipped out, all of a sudden, we had animals in our camp. And and the reason we had horses and cows and chickens and oxen was because there was a city in Czechoslovakia called Lidice. This was the city-- after Heydrich was assassinated in Prague, the Germans took the city, the small city, and killed all the males. All the men were killed in the camp, all the women and children were put in a concentration camp. The children were put to-- into-- young children were put into German families so they completely forgot what they were. And this was a farming community and since we were the nearest camp there, they transport all the animals-- the horses and-- into Theresienstadt. So-- [DRINKS] Now all of a sudden, everybody gets shipped out. We're down to about 20,000 people. This is a camp a week earlier, we were 75,000 people. All of a sudden, we're down to 15,000, 18,000 people-- I don't know the exact [INAUDIBLE]. All of a sudden, I got assigned to the stable because I-- I didn't operate-- I handled horses in Denmark on a farm so that Henry, my buddy, we get all-- all-- all of a sudden, all of us, the old gang now get the great jobs. Because all the initial teamsters, the guy who had these now were transporting bread. I had a team of horses. Every morning, I took-- I picked up all the dead bodies, put them into the crematory, and delivered them there. And-- and in the afternoon, I might have transported bread. I had another break. Occasionally, I went out with one of the SS to-- to the nearest city outside where he picked up rations for himself in the-- in the NCO club or whatever they had there. And-- and occasionally, I had a good one-- he gave me a piece of sausage or something like that. And if you had a bad one, he hit you a couple of times just to keep you straight. But in principle, it was a good job. The problem was we-- we saw the SS, the commandant, every day. Because A, he had a horse he was riding, a black horse. I think it was one of the oldest horses in the world because it was-- it was not a wild horse. So every day, he used to come to the stable. And sometimes he used to come early inspecting. We had to clean all the horses and you scraped them with that scraper and a brush. And then you hit the brush on the cement floor and then you had to have like 10 lines. And if you didn't have 10 lines, you were in trouble. So you really, really had to clean the horse. You couldn't take those lines away till about 11, 12 o'clock, you know. But basically, because we had an old fashioned sergeant, a Jewish guy who was a Sergeant in the Czechoslovakian cavalry, he was in charge of the stables.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he also took care of Commandant [INAUDIBLE] horse, which he almost rode every day. So that means every day we saw the lousy commandant. That means we never know what the son of a bitch was going to do, right? Because these people are unpredictable and there's-- let's be realistic about it, they had open season. You know, they could kill you. They don't even have to put a report in. Why did you kill him? Right? So nothing. So-- but our jobs were good, you know. We had-- we went out with the horses, we picked up the people. And on one day when I pick up the-- pick up the dead bodies, I see a couple of nurses. And I says, why are you here? Says, well, Professor so-and-so is died. That was the same professor who gave me the last shot of serum he had. And that-- because I never-- I-- I never was interested in who was there, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was just to meet another load I had to bring over and deliver and-- and go back. But this was the first time I started realizing those were people in there. I mean, people like me and everybody else who died, you know. And this was-- this was one of the greatest scientists, as I understand. He was in charge of the children diseases at the University of Prague. He was really one of the great, great professors and doctors the world has ever seen. We had-- Theresienstadt was something very unique, you know. It-- we had actors who put on plays at night in the barracks upstairs. And unfortunate, I was too young to really appreciate all this talent. This was such-- such-- and plus, I was so goddamn tired working these-- you know, because we had to get up at four, five o'clock in the morning to clean the horses and then-- then putting them-- you know, put them on a wagon and go back. And then we came back for lunch, take the horses. They ate better than we did. Because then we fed them-- and I used to grab a little oats occasionally, put it in a pocket and-- and do something with it, I don't remember. We put it and boiled it. It tasted horrible, at least it filled your stomach, you know. But-- so we-- we took food from the horses. We said, the hell with it. Eat straw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was-- there were so many opportunities to-- to-- to-- to partake there. I remember one thing I saw an operetta. Because the reason I saw there was a Danish comedian was playing in it. It was called 'The Fleidermouse' or 'The Bat, by Johann Strauss. And without costumes, without anything, but it was done so beautiful. Then by the way, they had a children opera. It was-- was composed by a very famous composer in-- in Theresienstadt, which is now classic, which is now being-- a lot of kids are doing it, you know, like it's a children opera.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a tremendous amount of talent because again, we had some of the greatest painters who got in trouble because they really painted all the-- the way it really was, besides what they did for the SS. So-- but in 1944, like the end of 1944-- September, October-- all bets were off. There were no more prominent Jews. All-- all of a sudden, the prominent Jews we used to have in that orchard was gone. Let me tell you what else we did in that orchard.

speaker: interviewer
text: We-- there was-- there was like a-- like a little building there, like one of those garden buildings like-- I don't know, like an open-- gazebo, like a gazebo all broken down. But underneath, we dug a big hole and we-- we put apples in there and pears in there. And all of a sudden, we don't go there anymore and it-- it gets locked up. The place gets locked up. And I pass it every time when I go outside the city with-- with the-- with the gendarmes, right? So one day I had a good gendarme. I says, I-- I think I left something over here. Can I jump over the fence and pick it up? I'm only going to be two minutes. And I think there's some apples in there, I'll bring you enough for yourself. He said, OK, jump over. I jumped over the fence, I opened this thing up, and the smell was killing me.

speaker: interviewee
text: All-- turned-- all were rotten, you know, because we hadn't been there for two or three months, you know. There was like-- so no more apples. And I mean, we-- we took tremendous chances, you know. I know I did. If you start realizing what-- what I did. I remember one time, I picked up 10 boxes of strawberries to bring into the commandant. Now, the-- Theresienstadt was very unique.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the middle, that's where the commandant was because that's where their barrack was and that's where their clubs were. Because the middle of the camp was never bombed, all right? So that's where they had their-- they had all the records there, they had-- the kasernes had name after countries. I was in the Hamburger Kaserne. Then there was Hanover and the-- and the-- What's a kaserne?

speaker: interviewee
text: A kaserne is a barrack. It's a permanent barrack. I'm sorry. Yeah. I thought-- those were known as kasernes, you know, permanent barracks, huge buildings. And so where was I?

speaker: interviewer
text: Talking about those huge barracks. You were-- You were in-- Everybody was in a different name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Was in different-- yeah. And the-- and the Germans had the Sudeten barracks. That's where they kept all the records from all over Europe was kept there, you know, where-- how many people and things like that. And also where the commandant was. So now I'm bringing in those 10 cases of strawberries and I look. There's no amount written on the slip.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had a guy with me. I says, two cases. Take off the minute we get inside the camp. Because we went inside the camp and then into the-- there was another gate. And he took off the two cases, two boxes, you know they're typical boxes. And I didn't realize what kind of a score that was. The people were crawling to me, give me a strawberry, give me a this and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how I got a wristwatch. I never in my life had a wristwatch. It was against the law anyway to have-- so I had a steel Omega, the ugliest thing-- I mean, today it would be the ugliest thing in the world. I couldn't wear it. I had to keep it in the barracks hidden. And so I wound up with an Omega watch, my first watch in my lifetime. And now I've got to tell you one thing. I was assigned after the-- after the ghetto start loosening up, you know, there was a-- I was assigned to a youth home.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, it was individual rooms. Let's say a room like this, as you see here, had maybe three-- three, six, nine. It had about 30 people in there with the-- with the beds on both sides-- three, one, two, three-- three, six, nine, 18, maybe 21 beds, right? 21 bunks. And I remember one time we had a kid in our barrack in this room and he was so sick he couldn't eat. He could not swallow anything, he was just wasting away but we kept him on report. And what we did that we didn't-- what we did with his bread was we toasted it. We didn't touch it, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I said to myself, I says, we knew the guy was going to guy-- die. I says, if he dies, let him die at night. Because at night, we could have grabbed the bread. In the daytime, the orderly would have grabbed it, the guy who was in charge of the whole floor. He died in the daytime and we didn't see the bread. But I tell you one thing, what I did, and I've been thinking about that. I got up one time in the middle of the night, I was so hungry. We had-- we had all this bread toasted there, you know, otherwise like it turned into tree bark, you know, whatever. And the bread, by the way, was like 80% sawdust, you know. So I stole two pieces of bread from him. This is the first time I ever stole something from another Jew. And-- and I'll tell you something, I felt real bad about it after I ate it. But he never ate it anyway. He never-- but the point of it-- we-- we kept it like, we didn't take anything while he was alive because we figured, let's-- let's [INAUDIBLE] if he gets-- so life goes on. Now all of a sudden, like November '44 all transports stop. We're now maybe 12,000 people in the camp. And it's filthy. All of a sudden, all this-- this garbage accumulates. There's nobody there to clean up. We had a tremendous war production on-- on-- on women. We had barracks out there where thousands of women used to do re-- re-- re-fixed uniforms, bad uniforms, fix them up. We had a factory there where 10,000-- where they-- where they used to do pitch. I don't know if you're familiar. Pitch is something you find in mines. You have to split it and it goes into electrical instruments like for submarines and things like that. And all of a sudden-- and I know because one of my jobs was to pick up the uniforms, bring them to the station, or pick up the pitch in boxes. And all of a sudden, there's nobody out there. These women are gone. We're down to a bare minimum of 10,000, 12,000 people. I don't know. And all of a sudden, no one transports. October-- I think November, no more transports. And we found out the Russians already-- we-- we still don't know what's going on. So one day, we got a transport of about 40 kids, young kids. So they go-- they're-- they're-- they're really in bad shape, you know. So I knew somebody in the hospital. So they bring them over to the hospital and the hospital had facilities to take showers. Kids didn't want to go in the shower room. We still don't know what the hell was going on. So now they clean these kids up, they-- you know, and they get shipped out again. We never knew where they went. And strange things happening. All of a sudden, it's now the beginning of '45 now, you know, like January, February.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of a sudden, an order comes down if somebody wants to volunteer for Switzerland. They're making up a transport of 500 people to go to Switzerland. Bullshit, right. Nobody volunteers. All of a sudden, some of us says, screw it, let's go. Let's-- let's put our name down. what-- what-- you know, what can happen, right? We volunteered for barracks building at one time. They-- they wanted volunteers to go out to build barracks for the German Army. Just to get out of camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all turned down. The Danish people, the Danish guys, were turned down. So now we were onto-- some of us volunteered. I don't know if I did it or not. We volunteered for to go on that trip. We get turned down. So they finally had to grab 500 people, and sure enough that transport went to-- went to Switzerland. This was when the war was almost over, right. So now it's life goes on. It's April 13th or 14th, I believe it was, 1945. The evening, we got a report-- we get-- we get an order from the Jewish elders. By the way, the original elder Epstein was shot in 1945 because he-- he protested the transports. So they made his second in command [INAUDIBLE]-- he just disappeared. He was shot in the basement of the commandant's office. And-- and then [INAUDIBLE] became the elder. He was a real piece of garbage. He was really one of the most hated people in the world. And Rabbi Beck was still there, he was still in the elders, and he was exempt from transport. We had a Danish rabbi, the chief rabbi. There was only one rabbi in Denmark. He went-- was also exempt, you know. And-- well, we didn't know why we were exempt. We just-- we just thought we were lucky not to go. And so [DRINKS] --we got notified on the night of April 13th, we have to be at six o'clock in the morning at the place what they used to call the appel, where you assemble for transport, all right. Nothing else-- there was no reason giving and everything. Now, one guy hid. One guy didn't report. And-- and you really can't hide in a place like that because-- unless you-- you have enough provision and things which nobody ever had, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this guy says, I don't trust these guys, you know. So he hid. And he didn't go and report. So next morning we stood there and they had-- had a row of tables set up and this time it was not Czechoslovakian guys. All SS were sitting there, checked our numbers against the list they had. And we still don't know what's going on, you know. So we were pushed over to one side there and then either around 9:30, 10 o'clock, we see the first Swedish buses pull into the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were told to board the buses, you know. And as we order-- get on the buses, there's a Swedish soldier hands you a Hershey bar and a pack of Chesterfields. We never smoked a cigarette before. As a kid, I once went in and stole a cigarette from my father, you know. Never smoked. I started smoking that time. So we got into the-- into the nice buses, you know, but they weren't like- they were more like school buses.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't have the reclining seats because they were interested in moving people. And on each bus there was a German soldier-- old guy, you know, because they didn't have a-- and the deal was, every time we went to a city, all the curtains had to be pulled down. We were not allowed to look out the window. Everything was smashed, right? So now-- what happened was on the second day, we were supposed to stop in Dresden. Overnight, there were-- the convoy had no provision for us to have food.

speaker: interviewee
text: All they gave us was Hershey bars. That's all they had, Hershey bars and cigarettes. They had provision for their own people. They had that small kitchen there, but they couldn't feed 400 people. That-- that was without any-- so now, we are supposed to stop over for the night at a place called-- a city called Dresden, right. So now right before we hit the city, like five, six o'clock in the morning, one of our buses breaks down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they couldn't leave it behind so they repaired it. So we were not allowed to drive at night because all things moving were under-- under Allied airplanes, you know. So you can't see a Swedish flag. So what happened was we pull about by maybe 10, 15 kilometers outside of-- of Dresden. And we're allowed to walk around outside the-- yeah, because you're still sitting. Now we're walking around, we're eating our chocolate. And we had so much chocolate we got sick and tired.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, at the-- at the-- at the Green Valley when I gave a speech, they gave me some Hershey bars. The kids went out and bought some Hershey bars because I talked about Hershey bars. So-- or one of the schools, I'm not sure which one it was. So that night, that city get bombed again, all right. And we could have been in that city. So again, we were saved, you know. The next day, we got on-- we got into the bus again, we drove to-- and at that time, we started to look the old man, the old soldier. We gave him some cigarettes so he- we could do anything we want. He was one of those [NON-ENGLISH], the one day drafted. He was like maybe 55, 60 or something, you know. So that's the kind of people at that time were-- [DRINKS] One thing happened one time. We are near Hamburg, also pull over at night because we're-- and-- and we're in a-- in a-- in a truck and there comes an SS division, Waffen-SS, you know, and with armored car. And this guy pulls right up next to us and stays there. So we heard this-- you know, when they were there, we weren't allowed to go outside. They didn't know we had the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So the guy-- the commandant of the Swedish transport-- goes over to the-- to the officer and says, please remove your troops from this spot here. We are neutral, we are under orders. The guy says, what are you, crazy? He says, I don't want to [INAUDIBLE]. So the guy says-- so the commandant says-- and this is-- I don't know how they could be that-- that-- that correct, but this guy says, well, if you don't I have to call Himmler, Vice Fuhrer Himmler, and tell him that you will not obey his orders. So when he heard that, the guy took off. But he could have-- at that time, you know, this is like 10, 15 days before the war is over. They were shooting people for no reason. He could have taken-- he could have turned around, let's wipe this convoy out and nobody would have known any different. But in the meantime, he got away with it. We went the next day, we crossed the Danish border.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for-- I don't know how did Danes find out about it, but all of a sudden, there's people standing on the streets with flowers and-- and-- and now, there was also a German commandant on this [INAUDIBLE]. So he says-- he says to the Swedish guy, if this does not stop, we will return. We'll turn around and go back to-- so they got hold of some of the Danish people there and says, look, got to stop and please call down the line that we can't have that kind of-- kind of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next-- that same evening-- that same afternoon, I think, we arrive in Copenhagen. We take the ferry from [INAUDIBLE] and we go all the way through and we-- we get on a ferry boat in Copenhagen and the next stop is Malmo, Sweden. And for all practical purpose, my war was over. We get assigned to a resort hotel which was empty at that time. And oh, I had that bathtub. I laid in that bathtub for I don't know how many hours, I guess to try to get rid of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the next day, we had some of the Jewish people came out there and got us clothes. And I got a suit and got a couple of shirts, some underwear things, like that, you know. And so now we looked like human people, like humans again. We were all-- we were all skinny in a sense, but we-- we survived, you know. And-- and so we stayed in Sweden there till-- they had a Danish brigade there at that time they were forming of Danish underground people. So we a bunch of us said, we'd like to join the brigade. But at that time, they said, it's too late. We are looking to get over there to Germany and things like that. So they didn't take us. Plus, we weren't in that kind of shape. But we had that kind of a hate left, you know. So about on the 15th of May, the war was over about eight days already, we get back to Denmark, right. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd you get back to Denmark?

speaker: interviewee
text: By they shipped us back, right. The Swedish take us back on a ferry. And at that time we get a sign for a short time, somebody-- big families in Denmark sort of adopt us for a short time, not for long. I-- I was assigned to the agricultural minister. You know, he's a member of the cabinet. And the first night, the butler comes up with the tray for breakfast and I-- I said, this is something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- it was like, I said, is this going to last forever? No, it wasn't. We only stayed there about a week or 10 days. Then they interviewed us, where would you like to be and things like that, you know. And I wanted to be an automobile mechanic so they assigned me-- they got me a job-- job, like an apprentice. You don't get paid for years. And I was assigned to a-- a orphanage for all elderly kids, you know, like not young kids. So everybody in this orphanage was, like, working or was learning something, you know. And so I was assigned to an orphanage and to a mechanic, automobile mechanic repair shop. And the first seven, eight, nine months, all I did was clean the floor. I didn't learn nothing. I said, this is ridiculous, you know. I've got to do that four years? So my-- my father's cousin finds me. How did he do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because the first thing you do when you come back is you put your name on-- on the Red Cross. You put your name down. And you put the name of your parents down and you have-- at that time, I didn't know my father was already in Israel. My father got liberated in 1943 in Italy and it was in a place called Cosenza, down near the boot. And it was liberated by the Jewish Brigade. And then even at that time, he had to be smuggled into Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't know who was alive, who wasn't. You see, that's when we start finding out what really happened, right. And still at that time, nobody knew it was in the millions, you know. We found out about gas chambers, but not in that kind of-- kind of-- we couldn't comprehend what they could do to us on a mass scale like that, you know. So-- I want to ask you a question. You talked before-- OK.
